title: Work as area: a variable force
scenario: A 2 kg block on a frictionless track starts at rest. A force that grows with distance, $F(x) = b\,x$ (here $b = 8$ N/m), pushes it for $d = 1.5$ m. How much work is done — and where does $\tfrac12 m v^2$ actually come from? Watch the work accumulate as the area under the force curve.

assumptions.claim: Frictionless track: the applied force is the only force doing work along the motion.
The force acts along the direction of motion (one dimension), so $W = \int F\,dx$ with no angle factor.
Point mass; the block starts from rest ($v_0 = 0$), so all the work becomes kinetic energy.
Ideal linear force law $F(x) = b\,x$ (the force is exactly proportional to displacement).

steps.label: What the algebra-based course hands you
steps.latex: W = F\,d \qquad(\text{constant force}) \qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad W = \bar F\,d,\ \ \bar F = \tfrac12 F_{\max}
steps.prose: For a constant force, work is force times distance. When the force *varies*, the algebra course patches it with an average force — which is only exact here because the force happens to rise linearly. It can compute the number but cannot say *why* the answer is an area.

work_done.label: Work done $W = \tfrac12 b d^2$ (the shaded area)

steps.label: Work is the area under the force–displacement curve
steps.prose: Work is defined as the integral of force over displacement. For a constant force this integral is a rectangle (and gives $W = F d$); for any force it is the area under the $F$–$x$ curve. The algebra formula is the special case where the area is a rectangle.

steps.label: Evaluate for the linear force $F(x) = b x$
steps.prose: The area under a straight line from the origin is a triangle: $\tfrac12\,(\text{base})\,(\text{height}) = \tfrac12\,x\,(b x) = \tfrac12 b x^2$. Its slope is $bx = F(x)$ — the force is the rate at which work accumulates.

steps.label: The kinetic energy falls out of the same integral
steps.latex: \int F\,dx = \int m\,\frac{dv}{dt}\,dx = \int m\,v\,dv = \tfrac12 m v^2 \ \Longrightarrow\ \tfrac12 m v^2 = W
steps.prose: Substituting $F = m\,dv/dt$ and $dx = v\,dt$ turns the work integral into $\int m v\,dv$. The memorized $\tfrac12 m v^2$ is not a separate fact — it is exactly the accumulated work. The area under the force curve IS the kinetic energy.

steps.label: So the speed follows from the area
steps.prose: Read the work off the area, set it equal to $\tfrac12 m v^2$, and the speed at any displacement follows. Drag the cursor: the shaded area and the kinetic energy move together because they are the same quantity.

proof.heading: The work is the area under the force curve — and the kinetic energy falls out of the integral.
proof.detail: check $\tfrac{d}{dx}W = F$; check $W = \int_0^x F\,du$; back-substitute into $\tfrac12 m v^2 = W$; collapse the constant-force case to $F\,d$

checks.claim: The work's slope is the force: $W'(x) = F(x)$ — the area's rate of growth is the curve's height.
The accumulated work is exactly the area: $W(x) = \int_0^x F\,du$.
The kinetic energy equals the work: $\tfrac12 m v^2 = W$ — the memorized $\tfrac12 m v^2$ is the area.
When the force is constant the integral collapses to $W = F\,d$ — the algebra formula is the area of a rectangle (the quadrature).

misconception.claim: Work is the maximum force times the distance, $W = F_{\max}\,d$.
misconception.refuted_by: The force is not constant — it climbs from $0$ to $F_{\max} = b\,d$. Work is the *area* under the $F$–$x$ line, a triangle of area $\tfrac12 F_{\max} d$ — exactly half the rectangle $F_{\max} d$. Drag the cursor: the shaded triangle, not the enclosing rectangle, is the work, and it equals the kinetic energy gained.

practice.asks: the work done by the variable force
practice.prompt: A force that grows with distance, $F = bx$ with $b = 8$ N/m, pushes a block from rest through $d = 1.5$ m. How much work does it do?

choices.misconception: Used $F_{\max}\,d$ — the full rectangle. The force ramps from $0$ up to $F_{\max} = bd$, so the area under $F(x)$ is the triangle $\tfrac12 bd^2$, half as much.
That is the peak force $F_{\max} = bd$ (in newtons), not the work (in joules). Work is the area under the force–distance curve.

algebra_steps.label: What the algebra-based course hands you
algebra_steps.latex: W = F\,d \qquad(\text{constant force}) \qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad W = \bar F\,d,\ \ \bar F = \tfrac12 F_{\max}
algebra_steps.prose: For a constant force, work is force times distance. When the force *varies*, the algebra course patches it with an average force — which is only exact here because the force happens to rise linearly. It can compute the number but cannot say *why* the answer is an area.

calculus_steps.label: Work is the area under the force–displacement curve
calculus_steps.prose: Work is defined as the integral of force over displacement. For a constant force this integral is a rectangle (and gives $W = F d$); for any force it is the area under the $F$–$x$ curve. The algebra formula is the special case where the area is a rectangle.

calculus_steps.latex: \int F\,dx = \int m\,\frac{dv}{dt}\,dx = \int m\,v\,dv = \tfrac12 m v^2 \ \Longrightarrow\ \tfrac12 m v^2 = W

calculus_steps.prose: Read the work off the area, set it equal to $\tfrac12 m v^2$, and the speed at any displacement follows. Drag the cursor: the shaded area and the kinetic energy move together because they are the same quantity.
